I like this one:

```python
import random
import enum
class RANDOM_ATTR(enum.EnumMeta):
    @property
    def RANDOM(self):
        return random.choice(list(self))


class Gender(enum.Enum,metaclass=RANDOM_ATTR): #this syntax works for python3 only
    FEMALE = 'female'
    MALE = 'male'


print(Gender.RANDOM)
```

Results:

```
>>> print(Gender.RANDOM)
Gender.MALE
>>> print(Gender.RANDOM)
Gender.MALE
>>> print(Gender.RANDOM)
Gender.FEMALE
>>> print(Gender.RANDOM)
Gender.FEMALE
>>> print(Gender.RANDOM)
Gender.FEMALE
```
This is really much more elegant, thanks.

I was trying to make two points:

1. Given `j > 0`, it's impossible for `(j - 1) / 2 = j`. Just analytically, if you solve `j = (j - 1) / 2` for `j`, you get `j = -1`. The only time this condition will be hit is when `j = -1` and that's impossible because you have a `j > 0` condition on the while loop.
2. If by some magic `i == j`, then `h.cmp(i, j)` is always false. So, the second part of the `if` condition still holds.

Either way, the `i == j` check isn't required.

Doing this as a callback is fragile, If the API call fails the cluster is effectively unavailable until failover is externally triggered. 

Also, there is no interlock to prevent racy updates. Imaginary scenario:

0. unhealthy cluster
1. n-0 acquires leader lock, starts callback.py
2. n-0 hangs for a period of time (IO system problems, network issues, etc.)
3. n-1 acquires leader lock, starts callback.py
4. n-1 callback.py completes, directing endpoint traffic to n-1
5. n-0 becomes active again, runs the API call and overwrites the endpoint with n-0
6. n-0 demotes itself
7. until next failover traffic is now routed to the standby.

I think this needs integration with the HA loop to fix properly.

What follows is my subjective opinion & experience:

More generally (not specific to these examples) it's good to default to using `const` because it encourages a functional/declarative style rather than imperative coding.

Two examples are the discussion above (https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/721#discussion_r92271869) and the filter logic below (https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/721#discussion-diff-92266163R156). Both of these use an imperative style, and tend to be a bit more brittle than functional/declarative logic because they rely on keeping track of internal state.

If you find yourself initializing a `let` variable and then updating the value in a loop or if statements, it's often worth exploring whether a more functional approach could be used.

Dynamic systems have stable and unstable fixed points. Without noise these systems would be driven to one of their stable fixed points and stay there for ever. So, fixed points resemble an equilibrium state. 

Noise pushes the system out of their equilibrium and the system's dynamics try to bring it back. So, by averaging the change of state we are able to model the deterministic dynamics as polynomial. Its roots are the fixed points of the dynamics. We are taking the largest fixed point. In the dissipative soliton example, this is an estimate for the equilibrium velocity of the dissipative soliton.

The peak memory usage actually matters since I have seen OOM in onnx-caffe2's CI :-)
And I don't think this change will increase any memory footprint. Using your example:
```
ModelProto modelProto = ...;
```
If we use unique_ptr, then the user would need to make a second copy and wrap that with a unique_ptr to pass into the optimizer, it has two copies during the whole time optimizer is running. While with my change, no matter whether ModelProto has move ctor or not, since we are passing model proto as const ref, it only has one copy, so it actually uses less memory.

I think returning a Status code is reasonable. While I agree that it is reasonable to assume that the model file has passed the checker before inference is invoked, it is still a good idea to safeguard the inference code against null-dereference etc. (E.g., this ensures that even if the op signature is changed to make an attribute optional, we don't fail with a null dereference.) Shape inference is (currently) viewed as optional (for optimization): there will be models where shape inference won't successfully infer shapes. For this reason, returning silently is a reasonable compromise for shape inference (in such edge cases). At least, that's my personal opinion.
